steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The LNH predicts an inverse relationship: more non-native speakers (higher exotericity) → lower grammatical complexity. Petrenko's team claims their data is “inconsistent with the LNH,” meaning their findings must show the opposite pattern. Since fusion and informativity are defined as positive indices of grammatical complexity, a finding that supports Petrenko must show fusion and informativity increasing with exotericity (a positive correlation).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Petrenko and colleagues' analysis showed a slightly positive correlation between grammatical complexity and fusion.: This only describes the relationship between complexity and fusion, not between either of those and exotericity. It doesn't address the LNH's core claim about exotericity at all. ✗
  • Choice B — Petrenko and colleagues' analysis showed a slightly negative correlation between grammatical complexity and exotericity.: A negative correlation between complexity and exotericity is exactly what the LNH predicts — this would support the LNH rather than contradict it. ✗
  • Choice C — Petrenko and colleagues' analysis showed a slightly positive correlation between fusion and exotericity and between informativity and exotericity.: Fusion and informativity are both positive indices of grammatical complexity. Positive correlations between each of them and exotericity means complexity rises with exotericity — the opposite of the LNH's prediction. This directly supports Petrenko's inconsistency claim. ✓
  • Choice D — Petrenko and colleagues' analysis showed a slightly negative correlation between grammatical complexity and fusion and between grammatical complexity and informativity.: This contradicts the passage, which defines fusion and informativity as positive indices of complexity — they should correlate positively with complexity by definition. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
